evidence_entities.text: Cushing's disease is a rare disorder characterized by overproduction of ACTH from a pituitary adenoma leading to hypercortisolemia that in turn leads to increased morbidity and mortality.
Recent evidence suggests that correction of hypercortisolemia may not lead to complete resolution of comorbidities associated with this condition.
In particular, increased cardiovascular risk may persist despite long-term remission of hypercortisolemia.

Multiple studies show that the standardized mortality ratio (SMR) is increased in CD (1.7–4.8), especially in patients with persistent hypercortisolism (3.7–4.2) compared to those in remission (1.8–3.17).
Cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events are the most common cause of death in CD.
Cardiovascular risk is increased in CD patients even 5 years after remission.
Hypertension occurs in 55–85 % of CD patients, but resolves after remission in only 44–75 %.
The prevalence of overt diabetes mellitus is 20–47 % and that of impaired glucose tolerance is 21–64 % in CD.
The prevalence of fractures and osteoporosis as assessed by dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) has been reported to be 15–50 and 38–50 % respectively.
Most (54–85 %) patients with CD have some psychiatric disturbance.
Hippocampal atrophy was seen in 27 % of 12 active CS patients and correlated with performance on cognitive tests.

notes: This review paper discusses the various comorbidities associated with Cushing's disease and their persistence even after treatment.
The paper emphasizes that cardiovascular risk may persist despite biochemical remission of hypercortisolemia.
Multiple comorbidities are described with specific prevalence rates provided from various studies.
